posts.postText:
Is "Full Stack" just... "Next.js"?

...I'm half-kidding.
But think about it.
My stack: React, Next, TS, Node, Mongoose, Tailwind.
With Next.js... I get:
✅ **Frontend:** React, TS, Tailwind
✅ **Backend:** API Routes
✅ **"DevOps":** Vercel handles it.

I still *need* to know Node.js (for the API logic).
I still *need* to know Mongoose/SQL (for the database).
...but "Next.js" is the *glue*. It's the *framework* for the *entire* stack.
It's the "Full Stack Operating System."

It's an amazing time to be a web dev.
One framework... to rule them all?

Do you see Next.js as a "frontend" tool? Or a "full stack" tool?

#nextjs #fullstackdeveloper #reactjs #webdev #javascript
posts.hook:
Is "Full Stack" just... "Next.js"?
...I'm half-kidding.
But think about it.

posts.postText:
Hot take: "MERN Stack" is an outdated term.

Think about it. Are you *really* just using "React"? No, you're using Next.js.
Is "Express" your main server? Or are you just using Next.js API routes?
Is "MongoDB" your only database? Or are you mixing in SQL or a vector DB for AI?

The stack is evolving. It's not a rigid acronym anymore. It's a hybrid, serverless-first, AI-integrated... *thing*.
We need a new name.

What do you call your "MERN" stack? I'm leaning towards "The Next.js Ecosystem".

#fullstackdeveloper #nextjs #mernstack #webdev #reactjs

posts.postText:
Fix video thumbnail loading issues with this simple trick!
Sometimes video tags struggle to load thumbnails, leaving users with blank spaces. This creates a disjointed user experience.
The trick is to briefly play the video for a tiny fraction of a second (0.01ms) and then immediately pause it. This forces the browser to load the first frame, effectively creating a thumbnail.

#WebDevelopment #VideoTag #UX #Frontend #CodingTips #TechTricks
posts.hook: Fix video thumbnail loading issues with this simple trick! Sometimes video tags struggle to load thumbnails, leaving users with blank spaces.

posts.postText:
Why your "simple" CSS is *so hard* to maintain.

You started simple.
Just a `styles.css` file.
Then... `header.css`. And `footer.css`.
Now... you have 50 CSS files.
...you have `.btn-primary` and `.button-blue`... and they *do the same thing*.
...you change one `h1`... and it breaks the layout on *another page*.

You don't have a "CSS" problem.
You have a "scoping" problem. And a "systems" problem.
This is why I *love* TailwindCSS.
It's not "CSS-in-JS." It's "Utility-first."
It *forces* a system. `mb-4` *always* means "1rem."
It's *scoped* to the component.
It's... maintainable.

Are you "pure CSS"? "Styled-Components"? "Tailwind"?
...Let's hear it.

#css #tailwindcss #frontend #webdesign #webdev
posts.hook:
Why your "simple" CSS is *so hard* to maintain.
You started simple.
Just a `styles.css` file.

posts.postText:
Your Next.js app probably has terrible SEO... even with Next.js.

I see this all the time. Devs think "I'm using Next.js, so my SEO is perfect!"
...then they wrap every piece of text in a `<div>` or `<span>`.
...they forget to add a unique `<title>` or `meta:description`.
...they have zero `<h1>` tags.

Next.js gives you the *tools* for SEO (like SSR and file-based routing). It doesn't do it *for* you.
Semantic HTML still matters. Content structure *still* matters.
I had to learn this fast on my ZyrixCraft project.

What's the most common "Next.js SEO" mistake you've seen?

#nextjs #seo #webdevelopment #fullstackdeveloper #reactjs

posts.postText:
Users don’t leave your product because it’s bad, they leave because it’s confusing. This can happen in under 10 seconds.

Here are 5 UI mistakes I keep seeing, even in funded startups:

❌ 1. Too many choices at once  
→ Users don’t explore; they freeze.  
❌ 2. No clear primary action  
→ If everything looks important, nothing is.  
❌ 3. Slow or cluttered onboarding  
→ First impression = last impression.  
❌ 4. Ignoring visual hierarchy  
→ Users shouldn’t “search” for what to do next.  
❌ 5. Designing for aesthetics, not behavior  
→ Pretty ≠ usable.  

What I’ve learned from building SaaS products is that good UI is not just about design; it’s about decision-making. Every screen should answer ONE question clearly: “What should the user do next?” If your product feels “clunky,” it’s probably not your tech; it’s your UX.

Curious, what’s the most frustrating UI you’ve used recently?  

#UIUX #ProductDesign #SaaS #Startup #SoftwareDevelopment #UserExperience #Founders #Hiring #DesignThinking #Tech
posts.hook: Users don’t leave your product because it’s bad, they leave because it’s confusing. This can happen in under 10 seconds.

posts.postText:
Designers: Stop asking for *impossible* animations.

I love you. I *am* you.
But that Dribbble shot you're referencing...
...it's a 10-second, pre-rendered After Effects video.
...it's not a *real*, interactive, responsive web component.

It doesn't account for:
➡️ `(prefers-reduced-motion)`
➡️ Poor network conditions
➡️ Main-thread blocking (jank!)
➡️ How it *actually* works with real, dynamic content

My "SparkVista" project was a balance. We *used* Locomotion... but we had to *tame* it.
The best animations are the ones that are *functional* and *fast*.
Not the ones that just look cool in a demo.

Devs: What's the most "impossible" animation you've been asked to build?

#frontend #uidesign #animation #webdesign #a11y
posts.hook: Designers: Stop asking for *impossible* animations. I love you. I *am* you.

posts.postText:
Let's talk about the *real* "Full Stack"...
...Frontend, Backend, and *Business*.

I see so many devs who are *allergic* to the "business" side.
They just want a ticket. They just want to code.
But working with REAL clients... you *can't* do that.

You have to be a *consultant*.
➡️ "You asked for this feature... but I think *this* feature will solve your problem *better*. Here's why."
➡️ "This will improve your SEO, which means *more leads*."
➡️ "We can A/B test this to *prove* it works."

When you start speaking "business"...
...you stop being a "coder."
...you become a *partner*.
...and *that's* how you get better projects (and better pay).

How comfortable are you talking "business" (ROI, SEO, conversions) with your clients/boss?

#fullstackdeveloper #softskills #freelancing #business #careeradvice

posts.postText:
I boosted my E-commerce app's Lighthouse score by 25 points. Here's how.
.
.
.
First, Code Splitting with React.lazy(). By default, React bundles all your code into one large file. This means users download everything, even pages they might never visit. React.lazy() lets you split your code, loading components dynamically only when they are needed. Pair it with React.Suspense to show a loading indicator, dramatically speeding up your initial page load.
.
Second, Image Optimization. Large images are a major bottleneck. Stop using bulky JPEGs and PNGs. Convert your images to modern formats like WebP or AVIF, which offer significantly smaller file sizes at the same quality. Use the <picture> tag to provide fallbacks for older browsers and add loading="lazy" to images "below the fold."
.
Finally, Memoization (useMemo/useCallback). This improves runtime performance, making your app feel faster. useMemo "remembers" the result of an expensive calculation, preventing it from re-running on every render. useCallback "remembers" a function, stopping unnecessary re-renders in child components that receive functions as props.
posts.hook: I boosted my E-commerce app's Lighthouse score by 25 points. Here's how.

posts.postText:
TypeScript's "any" type is a loaded gun.

I get it. You're in a hurry. That third-party library has awful types. You just... `const data: any = ...`.
You've *technically* satisfied the compiler. But you've thrown away the *entire* reason for using TypeScript.
You've just planted a runtime-error timebomb for your future self.

It's a failure of imagination.
A little bit of `unknown` + type-guarding...
Or a quick `zod` schema...
...is *always* better than `any`.

What's your "go-to" alternative for `any` when you're in a hurry?

#typescript #reactjs #cleancode #webdeveloper #fullstack

The market needs experience from freshers. It's not which isn’t obvious. 
Job before graduation,
It’s hard; sometimes, somewhere in the corner of mind, questions the decision. But in my case, the decision turned me into a different person, more mature and more perseverant. Gaining really valuable industry experience.
.
We are in a market where the “haua” [hype] of AI is shifting the landscape. But honestly, AI is an advantage. It’s impossible (and unnecessary) to cram the syntax of every single language now because AI can handle that. Instead, developers get to focus on what actually matters: logic, system flows, and optimizations, rather than just trying to resolve Promises using async/await.
.
Through all these, the question flips, it’s not an option to have a job/experience in the industry before graduation.

posts.postText:
Hot take: The "Full Stack Developer" is a myth.

...or at least, the "50/50" full-stack dev is.
Everyone *leans*.
I'm a "full stack" dev. But my heart is in the *frontend* and the *UX*.
I love building a "luxury" UI. I love "pixel-perfect" design.
I *can* (and do) build robust Node.js APIs and Mongoose schemas... but it's not what gets me *excited*.

And that's *okay*.
Know your strengths. Be a "T-shaped" developer.
Go *deep* on what you love (for me, UI/UX/React).
And be *broad* enough to handle the rest (Node, Mongo, SQL, DevOps).

What's your "T-shape"? Are you frontend-leaning or backend-leaning?

#fullstackdeveloper #webdev #reactjs #nodejs #careeradvice
posts.hook: Hot take: The Full Stack Developer is a myth.

posts.postText:
What does "Version Control (Git/GitHub)" *really* mean on a resume?

It *doesn't* mean "I know how to `git push`."
It means:
✅ I know how to *untangle* a merge-conflict.
✅ I know how to *write* a PR description.
✅ I know *why* `git rebase` is better than `git merge`.
✅ I know how to *review* someone else's code.
✅ I know what "atomic commits" are.

It means "I know how to be a *teammate*."
It's the single most important "UI/UX" skill...
...the "User Experience" of *working with you*.
My `https://lnkd.in/eQ4dbRJi (if that's your GH!) is my *other* portfolio.

What's one "git" skill you think *every* dev needs?

#git #github #versioncontrol #webdeveloper #cleancode

posts.postText:
"Not all who wander are lost, but some who are lost... find a 404 page."

There's something beautiful about error pages. They're the digital equivalent of a fork in 
the road – unexpected, slightly disorienting, but oddly honest.

Most sites treat 404s like embarrassments. I treat mine like an opportunity. An opportunity 
to say: "Hey, this page doesn't exist, but you do. And that matters more."

Built my portfolio with this mindset – even the errors should feel intentional.

Because great UX isn't just about what works. It's about what happens when things don't.

Live at: https://lnkd.in/eaGV8eEw

#UXDesign #WebDev #Philosophy #DesignThinking

posts.postText:
Let's talk about "technical debt."
...Sometimes, it's *worth it*.

I know, I know. "Clean code" is my mantra.
But "business" (that Commerce degree!) is about *tradeoffs*.
Sometimes... you *need* to ship.
You *need* to hit that deadline.
You *need* to get that A/B test *live*.

So... you take on a *little* debt.
You write the "good enough" code.
...and you add a `// TODO: Refactor this!` comment.
...and you *actually* make a ticket to refactor it.

"Technical debt" is fine.
It's "technical *bankruptcy*"—when you *never* pay it back—that kills you.
It's a *tool*, not a sin.

How do you *manage* your technical debt?

#cleancode #business #coding #webdeveloper #fullstack
posts.hook:
Let's talk about "technical debt."
...Sometimes, it's *worth it*.
I know, I know. "Clean code" is my mantra.

posts.postText:
🚨 Anthropic Claude Mythos Breach: A Wake-Up Call for AI Security
Anthropic is investigating unauthorized access to its cybersecurity AI model, Claude Mythos.

A group reportedly accessed it via a third-party contractor portal not maliciously, but enough to raise serious concerns.

Why it matters:
 Claude Mythos is built to detect software vulnerabilities and strengthen cybersecurity. It was limited to partners like Amazon, Microsoft, Apple, Cisco, and Mozilla, where it helped uncover 271 Firefox vulnerabilities.
Big takeaway:
-AI security is now a real risk surface.
-AI models are becoming high-value targets
-Tools for defense can also enable AI-powered cyber threats

The question isn’t “Can AI secure systems?”
It’s “Who secures the AI?”
#Anthropic #ClaudeMythos #AISecurity #Cybersecurity #ArtificialIntelligence #TechNews #InfoSec
posts.hook: Anthropic Claude Mythos Breach: A Wake-Up Call for AI Security

posts.postText:
State Management in React is a solved problem.
...and the solution is *not* Redux. (For most apps.)

Okay, hot take.
I've used Redux. I've used Context.
But for 90% of my projects? I use `Zustand`.
Why?
➡️ **It's simple.** No "Providers." No "Actions." No "Reducers."
➡️ **It's minimal.** Tiny bundle size.
➡️ **It's "un-opinionated."** It doesn't *care* how you structure your store.
➡️ **It handles `async`...** without 500 lines of "thunk" boilerplate.

`useContext` is great... until your whole app re-renders.
Zustand is "Context" done *right*.
It's my default choice.

What's your *go-to* state-manager for React in 2025?

#reactjs #statemanagement #javascript #frontend #zustand
posts.hook:
State Management in React is a solved problem.
...and the solution is *not* Redux. (For most apps.)
Okay, hot take.

posts.postText:
Stop naming your functions `handleData`.

...or `doThing`. Or `manager`.
This is "clean code 101."
It tells me *nothing*.
What data? What thing? What does it manage?

Good Naming:
➡️ `fetchUserById`
➡️ `validatePasswordStrength`
➡️ `calculateCartTotal`
➡️ `UserAuthMiddleware`

A good function name describes *exactly* what it does.
It's the *first* piece of documentation.
If you need a comment to explain your function's name...
...you need a *better* function name.

What's the *worst* (or funniest) variable/function name you've ever seen in a real codebase?

#cleancode #javascript #typescript #coding #webdeveloper
posts.hook:
Stop naming your functions `handleData`.
...or `doThing`. Or `manager`.
This is "clean code 101."

posts.postText:
"Responsive Design" is not just 3 breakpoints.

It's not "mobile, tablet, desktop."
It's *fluid*.
I see so many sites that look great at 375px...
...and 1440px...
...but are *completely* broken at 900px.

Why? They only tested the "device" presets.
My trick?
I just... resize my browser.
Slowly.
The *second* it looks weird... *that's* my new breakpoint.
I'm not designing for "an iPhone." I'm designing for "a screen."
(Tailwind's `md:`, `lg:` prefixes make this *so* easy to manage.)

Do you use "device" breakpoints? Or "fluid" breakpoints?

#responsivedesign #css #frontend #webdesign #tailwindcss
posts.hook:
"Responsive Design" is not just 3 breakpoints.
It's not "mobile, tablet, desktop."
It's *fluid*.

posts.postText:
Your MERN stack app is probably slower than it needs to be.
.
We all love the MERN stack, but it’s easy to create bottlenecks. My biggest "aha" moment was realizing how lazy querying in Mongoose can kill performance.
.
I see devs fetching entire documents when they only need two fields. Or worse—doing a .find() inside a .map() loop. That's an N+1 query problem, and it's a performance disaster.
Use .select() to grab only what you need. Learn to love $lookup and aggregation pipelines. Your database—and your users—will thank you.
What’s the worst Mongoose/MongoDB mistake you’ve ever made (or seen)? We’ve all been there.
#fullstackdeveloper #mongodb #nodejs #webperformance #mern
posts.hook:
Your MERN stack app is probably slower than it needs to be.
.

posts.postText:
Figma vs. Code: Who is the *real* source of truth?

I've lived this battle. I'm both a dev and a UI/UX designer.
The designer builds a "pixel-perfect" Figma file.
The dev builds it.
...then a change is needed.
Does the designer update Figma? Or does the dev just... change the code?

A month later, Figma is a graveyard of old ideas. The *real* design lives in the TailwindCSS config and the React components.
My solution: The "source of truth" *must* be the code.
Figma is for *prototyping* and *ideation*.
The "component library" in Storybook or in the app *is* the design system.

Where does the "source of truth" live for your team?

#figma #uidesign #frontend #reactjs #tailwindcss
posts.hook:
Figma vs. Code: Who is the *real* source of truth?
I've lived this battle. I'm both a dev and a UI/UX designer.

posts.postText:
We have reached a point where I trust imperfect humans more than perfect AI responses. 
 
Recently, I had a conversation on LinkedIn about startup challenges, and the responses were impressively smart, almost too perfect. Every message felt like it came straight out of a textbook


Those are the things that create meaningful conversations

So, I said, If you use less AI, I will be more interested in chatting with you 
 
I don’t dislike AI, in fact, I think it is a powerful tool. But I have noticed that as it gets easier to generate polished answers, original thinking becomes more valuable. 
 
I remember the people who shared their real experiences, not those who provided perfectly formatted replies. 
 
AI can enhance your communication skills, but it should not replace your unique voice. In a world where everyone is using the same AI tool, your own perspective is more valuable. 

Use AI, but do not let it overshadow your individuality. 

What do you think?
posts.hook: We have reached a point where I trust imperfect humans more than perfect AI responses.

posts.postText:
Code is jazz. And I've been learning to improvise. 🎷

Started with the basics (HTML, CSS, JS – the standard sheet music).
Then learned to riff (React, Next.js, TypeScript).
Added my own style (performance optimization, UI/UX design).
And now? I'm jamming. (Full-stack apps, admin dashboards, the whole set).

Just dropped my "personal introduction" page – think of it as my 
liner notes. The story behind the music. The progression from 
beginner to "hey, I actually know what I'm doing now."

It's got:
→ My career phases (from foundation to professional practice)
→ Core principles (like why I obsess over details)
→ Skills breakdown (frontend, backend, the works)
→ Even my interests (because devs are human too)

Not trying to be the next tech virtuoso. Just want to make good music 
and vibe with fellow musicians.

🎵 Listen in: https://lnkd.in/eQMYPDyX

#WebDev #Coding #FullStackDeveloper #TechLife
posts.hook: Code is jazz. And I've been learning to improvise. 🎷 Started with the basics (HTML, CSS, JS – the standard sheet music).

posts.postText:
Let's talk about the "Edge".
...No, not the browser. Edge *computing*.

This is one of the *coolest* things in Next.js right now.
We all know "Serverless" (runs in one region).
But "Edge" functions... they run *everywhere*.
They're "serverless functions" that run at the datacenter *closest to your user*.

What can you do with it?
✅ **Blazing-fast personalization:** Show a US user different content than an Indian user.
✅ **Super-low-latency redirects.**
✅ **A/B testing... at the *network level*.**

It's a new way of thinking. You're not just "frontend" or "backend." You're... "everywhere."
I'm *so* excited to build with this.

What's the *coolest* use-case you've seen for Edge functions?

#nextjs #edgecomputing #serverless #fullstackdeveloper #webdev
posts.hook:
Let's talk about the "Edge".
...No, not the browser. Edge *computing*.

posts.postText:
The Experience Paradox: Why are we asking Interns for 3 years of experience? 
.
To the Human Resources leaders, Recruiters, and Hiring Managers shaping the workforce:
We are facing a vicious cycle in the recruitment landscape today. When a Fresher applies for a job, the rejection is standard: "You lack experience." 
But when they pivot to apply for Internships to gain that experience, the response is often the same.
No Internship = No Experience. No Experience = No Job.
So, how does a skilled Full Stack Developer or a creative graduate break into the industry?
Thousands of educated candidates proficient in React, NodeJS, and Web Development sit idle, not because they lack skill, but because they lack a "corporate stamp" on their resume. 
Meanwhile, we often see roles filled based on referrals rather than raw technical capability.
Freshers aren't asking for shortcuts or sympathy. We are asking for a platform.
To the companies looking for the next unicorn employee: Remember that every Senior Developer, CTO, and Manager was once a fresher who just needed one opportunity.
If you close the door on fresh talent today, you stifle the experts of tomorrow.
Let’s shift the hiring culture. Let’s value Potential and Portfolio over years on paper. Give us a chance to code, design, and deliver. You might just find your best asset.
👇 HRs, let’s discuss: Are entry-level standards becoming unrealistic?
.
#Hiring #Freshers #EntryLevel #Recruitment #FullStackDeveloper #WebDevelopment #Frontend #Backend #JavaScript #ReactJS #NodeJS #SoftwareEngineering #TechTalent #OpenToWork #MumbaiJobs #Developers #GenZWorkforce #HumanResources #TalentAcquisition

posts.postText:
I'm a "developer artist." Here's my manifesto.

To me, code is just a medium. Like paint, or clay.
"Web developer" is the job title. "Artist" is the *approach*.

➡️ **It's not just "functional."** It has to be *beautiful*. (My UI/UX focus).
➡️ **It's not just "fast."** It has to be *smooth*. (My obsession with animation & performance).
➡️ **It's not just "done."** It has to be *elegant*. (My belief in clean code & smart architecture).

I built the "SparkVista" site not just as a "frontend dev," but as a designer.
I'm not here to just... translate tickets.
I'm here to build *cool and useful websites*.

Are you a "developer," an "engineer," an "artist," or something else?

#personalbranding #uidesign #webdeveloper #fullstackdeveloper #passion
posts.hook: I'm a "developer artist." Here's my manifesto.

posts.postText:
Developers, you're designers too. Here are 3 UI/UX principles I learned from my High-End Real Estate project.
.
.
.
Your design might look busy, but it's costing you conversions.
.
A "premium" feel isn't an accident. It's a choice.
.
Users decide if your site is credible in milliseconds, often before they even read your headline. A cluttered, confusing layout screams "unprofessional.
.
.

-[BAD ❌]
*A box with all text the same size and weight. It's hard to know where to look.
*Headline
*Some paragraph text that is just as big.
*Button

+[GOOD ✅]
*A box with clear differences in font weight and size.
*Main Headline (Large & Bold)
*Supporting subtext (Smaller & lighter)
*[ A Clear Button ]




#uiux #webdesign #figma #webdevelopment #uxdesign #uidesign
posts.hook: Developers, you're designers too. Here are 3 UI/UX principles I learned from my High-End Real Estate project.

posts.postText:
A year ago I was debugging auth at 2am for a startup I'd never met in person.
No call. No intro. Just a Vercel link, and a "lmk when it's done."
I was 18. Working from Kolkata. They were in Netherlands.
And the wildest part? They trusted me completely.
No micromanagement. No "are you sure you know what you're doing?" Just results, async, across time zones.
That freedom felt incredible for about 4 days.
Then the loneliness hit.
No team standup to complain to. No one to notice when I shipped something clean. No "nice work" that wasn't a Slack emoji at 11pm.
Freelancing as a teenager teaches you discipline fast.
It also teaches you that autonomy and isolation look identical from the outside.
I kept shipping. Built their entire auth flow, fixed 3 prod bugs they didn't know existed, handed it off clean.
Got paid.
Never heard from them again.
That's the gap nobody talks about. The trust is real. The work is real. The loneliness is also real.
I don't say this to complain. I say it because I see a lot of "freelance = freedom" takes and very few honest ones.
Has remote work ever felt isolating to you?
#Freelancing #RemoteWork #BuildingInPublic
posts.hook: A year ago I was debugging auth at 2am for a startup I'd never met in person. No call. No intro.

posts.postText:
"Just use Vite. You don't need Next.js."
I hear this advice a lot. And honestly? It's dangerous. Yes, Vite is faster to set up. Yes, it's great for Single Page Apps (SPAs).
But in 2025, clients don't just want an "App." They want SEO. They want OG tags for social sharing. They want blazing fast initial loads (SSR). They want API routes built-in.
If you start with Vite, you eventually end up manually building a router, installing an SEO library, and setting up a separate Express server.
You end up "reinventing" Next.js, but worse.
Next.js isn't "overkill." It's "future-proofing."
Unless I'm building a dashboard behind a login wall, I’m defaulting to Next. Every. Single. Time.
Is there any scenario where you'd still choose a plain CRA/Vite setup over Next.js?
#webdevelopment #nextjs #vite #frontend #coding

posts.postText:
Confession: I still use Adobe Photoshop.

I know, I know. Figma is my daily driver for UI. FigJam is for whiteboarding.
But when I need to do *real* image manipulation for a client?
➡️ Creating complex textures for a "luxury" site.
➡️ Editing a CEO's headshot.
➡️ Compositing 3 images into one hero banner.
...Figma just can't do it.

Knowing how to *properly* edit, composite, and export assets from Photoshop is a seriously underrated skill for a "full stack" dev who also... *designs*.
It's part of the "artist" toolkit.

What's one "old" tool you refuse to give up?

#photoshop #uidesign #webdesign #figma #fullstack
posts.hook:
Confession: I still use Adobe Photoshop.
I know, I know. Figma is my daily driver for UI. FigJam is for whiteboarding.

posts.postText:
Let's talk about the "T" in your stack: TypeScript... or Tailwind?

For me, the "T" in my stack (React, Next.js, Node.js) has become TailwindCSS.
I know, I know. TypeScript is crucial. I use it on every project.
But... Tailwind has *fundamentally* changed *how* I build and *think* about UI.

It connected my Figma designs directly to my code.
It forced a "systems-first" approach.
It made "responsive design" a joy instead of a chore.
TypeScript makes my app *robust*. Tailwind makes my app *beautiful* (and maintainable).

If you *had* to give one up on your next project... which "T" would it be?

#tailwindcss #typescript #frontend #reactjs #webdev
